content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

SUPERSEDED
IN PART
by: 94-045

January 2, 1991

Donald G. Freeman
P.O. Box 805
Carmel by the Sea, CA 93921

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-90-664

Dear Mr. Freeman:

This is in response to your request on behalf of Planning
Commissioner Brian Congleton for advice regarding his duties under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹ Because a specific governmental decision is not
pending, we will provide you with informal advice pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c).²

QUESTIONS

1. Mr. Congleton is an architect who serves on both the
Planning Commission and the Design Review Committee. May he
present his architectural plans to the agencies?

2. If under the Act Mr. Congleton is limited in his ability
to present his plans and communicate with staff, is it permissible
for him to hire or associate with another person to appear before,
and present his plans to, the agencies?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Mr. Congleton may prepare plans to submit to either
agency but he may not appear before or have any contact with the

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-90-664
Page 2


other planning commissioners regarding his plans. He may
communicate with staff only as is necessary concerning the
processing or evaluation of the plans, and regarding matters which
are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual or clerical.

2. Mr. Congleton may not hire or associate with another
person to present his plans before the agencies because that would
constitute an attempt to influence the planning commissioners.

FACTS

Brian Congleton is a member of the Carmel-by-the-Sea's
Planning Commission. He is also a member of the Design Review
Committee, which consists of three planning commissioners
appointed by the planning commission. Mr. Congleton is a sole
practicing architect and wishes to represent clients before the
design review committee and the planning commission.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 provides that no public official shall make,
participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he knows or
has reason to know has a financial interest. An official has a
financial interest if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on any source of income
aggregating $250 or more provided to the public official within 12
months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section
87103(c).)

A member of a planning commission is a public official.
(Section 82048; see also West Advice Letter, No. 88-413, copy
enclosed.) Presumably Mr. Congleton has received over $250 in
income from each client within the 12 months prior to the
presentation of the plans. Accordingly, he has a financial
interest under Section 87103(c).

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when he or
she votes on a matter, obligates the agency to a course of action,
or enters into any contractual agreement on behalf of the agency.
(Regulation 18700(b), copy enclosed.)³ An official "attempts to
use his or her official position to influence a decision" if the
official contacts, appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. (Regulation 18700.1(a), copy enclosed.)


3        Regulation 18700(c) (copy enclosed), which defines
"participates in the in the making of a governmental decision,"
does not appear to apply to Mr. Congleton's situation.

Our File No. I-90-664
Page 3


Thus under 18700.1(a), Mr. Congleton may not appear before or
"otherwise attempt to influence any member" of either the planning
commission or the design review committee. Two exceptions to this
rule apply to architects. The first exception provides:

...Notwithstanding subsection (a) an official is not
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision of an agency . . . if the official:
***
(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an
architectural, engineering or similar nature to be used by a
client in connection with a proceeding before any the agency.
However, this provision applies only if the official has no
other direct oral or written contact with the agency with
regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except
for necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings or submissions
prepared by the official.

Regulation 18700.1(b)(4).

This exception would permit Mr. Congleton to prepare
architectural plans for both the planning commission and the
design review committee. However, under this exception, he would
be limited to "necessary contact with agency staff concerning the
processing or evaluation of the drawings." This means he may
respond to staff questions relating to the evaluation of drawings
or relating to their movement through approval process. (See
Holbert Advice Letter, No. I-90-80, copy enclosed.) He may also
have contacts regarding matters which are solely ministerial,
secretarial, manual or clerical. (Regulation 18700(d)(1); Holbert
Advice Letter, supra.) He may not contact, appear before, nor
answer questions from other members of the planning commission or
design review board. (Levinger Advice Letter, I-88-328, copy
enclosed.)

The second exception provides:

...Notwithstanding subsection (a) an official is not
attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
governmental decision of an agency . . . if the official:

(5) Appears before a design or architectural review
committee or similar body of which he or she is a member to
present drawings or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature which the official has prepared
for a client if the following three criteria are met:

(A) The review committee's sole function is to review
architectural or engineering plans or designs and to make
recommendations in that instance concerning those plans or
designs to a planning commission or other agency;

Our File No. I-90-664
Page 4


(B) The ordinance or other provision of law requires
that the review committee include architects, engineers or
persons in related professions, and the official was
appointed to the body to fulfill this requirement; and

(C) The official is a sole practitioner.

Regulation 18700.1(b)(5).

This second exception applies only to design review boards.
If applicable, the official may appear before the committee and
communicate with committee members without the restrictions
imposed under 18700.1(b)(4). You do not provide enough
information in your letter to determine whether the design review
committee meets the three criteria specified in 18700.1(b)(5).
Even assuming it does, however, Mr. Congleton could not present
drawings to it because the other members of the committee are
planning commissioners and Mr. Congleton would be "attempting to
influence" the design review committee members under 18700.1(a).
(See West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413, copy enclosed.) If the
planning commission has already reviewed the plans and will not
under any circumstances review them again, and if the exception of
18700.1(b)(5) otherwise applies, Mr. Congleton may then present
the plans to the design review committee and communicate with its
members.

Mr. Congleton has also requested information regarding his
hiring an employee or associating with another architect so that
such employee or architect could present Mr. Congleton's plans to
the commission and design review committee. Under 18700.1(a),
this would constitute "attempting to influence" because the term
includes "contacts, appears before, or otherwise attempts to
influence." The term is construed broadly, as can be seen from
the Levinger Advice Letter, supra, where an architect was not
permitted to "ghostwrite" materials for a developer to present to
the planning commission, and in the Duffel Advice Letter (I-90-
390, copy enclosed), where a public official was "attempting to
influence" simply by arranging a meeting between two public
officials. Accordingly, under any of the scenarios Mr. Congleton
proposes, by arranging for another person to present his plans to
either the commission or the design review committee, he would be
"attempting to influence" the other planning commissioners in
violation of the Act.

Our File No. I-90-664
Page 5


I trust this letter adequately addresses your questions. If
you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel
free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Peggy Bernardy
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:PB:dg

Enclosures

(Regulation 18700.1(a), copy enclosed.)
